analogy: Think of "Organisms and Populations" through the chapter's own opening images: Diversity is not only a characteristic of living organisms but also of content in biology textbooks. Biology is presented either as botany, zoology and microbiology or as classical and modern. The latter is a euphemism for molecular aspects of biology. Luckily we have many threads... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: RAMDEO MISRA, CHAPTER 11, ORGANISMS AND POPULATIONS, 11.1 Populations. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Organisms and Populations is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that diversity is not only a characteristic of living organisms but also of content in biology textbooks.

The text explains that biology is presented either as botany, zoology and microbiology or as classical and modern.

The text explains that the latter is a euphemism for molecular aspects of biology.

The text explains that luckily we have many threads which weave the different areas of biological information into a unifying principle.

The text explains that ecology is one such thread which gives us a holistic perspective to biology.

The text explains that the essence of biological understanding is to know how organisms, while remaining an individual, interact with other organisms and physical habitats as a group and hence behave like organised wholes, i.e., population, community, ecosystem or even as the whole biosphere.

The text explains that a particular aspect of this is the study of anthropogenic environmental degradation and the socio-political issues it has raised.

The text explains that this unit describes as well as takes a critical view of the above aspects.

The text explains that chapter 11 Organisms and Populations Chapter 12 Ecosystem Chapter 13 Biodiversity and Conservation

The text explains that ramdeo Misra is revered as the Father of Ecology in India.

The text explains that born on 26 August 1908, Ramdeo Misra obtained Ph.D in Ecology (1937) under Prof.

The text explains that he established teaching and research in ecology at the Department of Botany of the Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi.

The text explains that his research laid the foundations for understanding of tropical communities and their succession, environmental responses of plant populations and productivity and nutrient cycling in tropical forest and grassland ecosystems.

The text explains that misra formulated the first postgraduate course in ecology in India.

The text explains that d degree under his supervision and moved on to other universities and research institutes to initiate ecology teaching and research across the country.

The text explains that he was honoured with the Fellowships of the Indian National Science Academy and World Academy of Arts and Science, and the prestigious Sanjay Gandhi Award in Environment and Ecology.

The text explains that due to his efforts, the Government of India established the National Committee for Environmental Planning and Coordination (1972) which, in later years, paved the way for the establishment of the Ministry of Environment and Forests (1984).

The text explains that we can try to understand its complexity by investigating processes at various levels of biological organisation–macromolecules, cells, tissues, organs, individual organisms, population, communities, ecosystems and biomes.

The text explains that at any level of biological organisation we can ask two types of questions – for example, when we hear the bulbul singing early morning in the garden, we may ask – ‘How does the bird sing ?’ Or, ‘Why does the bird sing ?’ The ‘how-type’ questions seek the mechanism behind the process while the ‘why- type’ questions seek the significance of the process.

The text explains that for the first question in our example, the answer might be in terms of the operation of the voice box and the vibrating bone in the bird, whereas for the second question the answer may lie in the bird’s need to communicate with its mate during breeding season.

The text explains that when you observe nature around you with a scientific frame of mind you will certainly come up with many interesting questions of both types - Why are night-blooming flowers generally white?

The text explains that cHAPTER 11 ORGANISMS AND POPULATIONS 11.1 Populations

The text explains that in this chapter we explore ecology at population levels.

The text explains that 11.1 POPULATIONS 11.1.1 Population Attributes In nature, we rarely find isolated, single individuals of any species; majority of them live in groups in a well defined geographical area, share or compete for similar resources, potentially interbreed and thus constitute a population.

The text explains that in earlier chapters you have learnt that although an individual organism is the one that has to cope with a changed environment, it is at the population level that natural selection operates to evolve the desired traits.

The text explains that if the age distribution (per cent individuals of a given age or age group) is plotted for the population, the resulting structure is called an age pyramid (Figure 11.1).

The text explains that whatever ecological processes we wish to investigate in a population, be it the outcome of competition with another species, the impact of a predator or the effect of a pesticide application, we always evaluate them in terms of any change in the population size.

The text explains that population size, technically called population density (designated as N), need not necessarily be measured in numbers only.

The text explains that although total number is generally the most appropriate measure of population density, it is in some cases either meaningless or difficult to determine.

The text explains that in an area, if there are 200 carrot grass (Parthenium hysterophorus) plants but only a single huge banyan tree with a large canopy, stating that the population density of banyan is low relative to that of carrot grass amounts to underestimating the enormous role of the Banyan in that community.

The text explains that if you have a dense laboratory culture of bacteria in a petri dish what is the best measure to report its density?

The text explains that for instance, the number of fish caught per trap is good enough measure of its total population density in the lake.

The text explains that we are mostly obliged to estimate population sizes indirectly, without actually counting them or seeing them.

The text explains that it keeps changing with time, depending on various factors including food availability, predation pressure and adverse weather.

The text explains that in fact, it is these changes in population density that give us some idea of what is happening to the population – whether it is flourishing or declining.

The text explains that whatever might be the ultimate reasons, the density of a population in a given habitat during a given period, fluctuates due to changes in four basic processes, Figure 11.1 Representation of age pyramids for human population

The text explains that 193 ORGANISMS AND POPULATIONS two of which (natality and immigration) contribute to an increase in population density and two (mortality and emigration) to a decrease.

The text explains that (i) Natality refers to the number of births during a given period in the population that are added to the initial density.

The text explains that figure 11.2 So, if N is the population density at time t, then its density at time t +1 is Nt+1 = Nt + [(B + I) – (D + E)] You can see from the above equation (Fig.

The text explains that 11.2) that population density will increase if the number of births plus the number of immigrants (B + I) is more than the number of deaths plus the number of emigrants (D + E).

The text explains that under normal conditions, births and deaths are the most important factors influencing population density, the other two factors assuming importance only under special conditions.

The text explains that (i) Exponential growth : Resource (food and space) availability is obviously essential for the unimpeded growth of a population.

The text explains that ideally, when resources in the habitat are unlimited, each species has the ability to realise fully its innate potential to grow in number, as Darwin observed while developing his theory of natural selection.

The text explains that if you are familiar with basic calculus, you can derive the integral form of the exponential growth equation as Nt = N0 ert where Nt = Population density after time t N0 = Population density at time zero r = intrinsic rate of natural increase e = the base of natural logarithms (2.71828) Any species growing exponentially under unlimited resource conditions can reach enormous population densities in a short time.

The text explains that (provided food and space remain unlimited) (ii) Logistic growth: No population of any species in nature has at its disposal unlimited resources to permit exponential growth.

The text explains that this leads to competition between individuals for limited resources.

The text explains that the governments of many countries have also realised this fact and introduced various restraints with a view to limit human population growth.

The text explains that in nature, a given habitat has enough resources to support a maximum possible number, beyond which no further growth is possible.

The text explains that a population growing in a habitat with limited resources show initially a lag phase, followed by phases of acceleration and deceleration and finally an asymptote, when the population density reaches the carrying capacity.

The text explains that name important defence mechanisms in plants against herbivory.

The text explains that an orchid plant is growing on the branch of mango tree.

The text explains that how do you describe this interaction between the orchid and the mango tree?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11: Organisms and Populations

11.1.3 Life History Variation Populations evolve to maximise their reproductive fitness, also called Darwinian fitness (high r value), in the habitat in which they live.
11.1.4 Population Interactions Can you think of any natural habitat on earth that is inhabited just by a single species?

Do not add outside examples to Organisms and Populations; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use RAMDEO MISRA and CHAPTER 11 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
